title: Uragoga fluminensis
taxonomy: Uragoga fluminensis is a species classified in the family Apocynaceae. This family consists of flowering plants commonly known as dogbane or oleander dogbane. The genus Uragoga is a group of plants native to the tropical regions of the world. Uragoga fluminensis is specifically found in the Amazon rainforest in South America. Its classification is part of a larger group of plants known as the Gentianales order.
uses: Uragoga fluminensis, also known as the Brazilian guava, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its potential health benefits. The plant's leaves and bark are rich in antioxidants and have been used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal issues. The extracts from Uragoga fluminensis have been reported to have anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antimicrobial properties. In some parts of Brazil, the plant is used to make a tea that is believed to aid in digestion and reduce inflammation. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy of Uragoga fluminensis for medicinal purposes.

parts: Uragoga fluminensis, commonly known as the Guanabanier, is a plant species native to the tropical regions of South America. The medicinal parts of Uragoga fluminensis, also known as Soursop, primarily include its leaves, roots, and fruit. The leaves of the plant are used to treat various ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. The roots of Uragoga fluminensis are often used to treat digestive issues and respiratory problems.
